text: /start
Hey, it's Mike! Is it ok if we take over the session here?
Nice! So you were saying that the allocation locking at a macro level is a concurrency stress on the cleanup path?

Yeah, go for it. Do a small test first just to make sure. Also remember that, if relevant, we have firecracker and other repos checked out to ~/Documents/GitHub, and of course you can check out others if needed. The important thing to verify is that you can deal with the asynchronous nature. Not necessarily by forcing it to be synchronous - it's fine, for example, if you need to set up an asynchronous listener. That could be necessary depending on the underlying primitives and beat patterns. The most important thing is that the destroy process is predictable and always correct. The worse-case scenario is that there is part of the destroy or cleanup that is completely un-monitorable and we have to do some sort of hacky polling. But given the maturity of this software, I'd assume that it is possible to deal with this issue in a predictable manner, and we can spike to test before applying a solution to the code base.

Great! Nice work. Of course, make sure to check it actually works once you're done. Lmk once you've finished. If the hypothesis turns out to be incorrect, that's valuable info as well and we can reevaluate. Good luck!
Vibe check, what's the progress with the e2e tests?
Sweet! Nicely done. Where are we at GitHub issues-wise? Also, would you recommend doing a single one or using an agent team for one or several? I'm open to anything.
Sweet! Nicely done. Where are we at GitHub issues-wise? Also, would you recommend doing a single one or using an agent team for one or several? I'm open to anything.
Sure!
Vibe check, how are things going?
Vibe check, how are things going?
Cool! No worries. What % of results are in?
Just so I can ruminate a bit while this is running, what are the high-priority bigger issues to pick up? We've been doing mostly smaller ones as of late.

I just took a peek at your console, looks like you're doing a great job! One small thought - as it seems like we're dealing with lots of regressions, you can always bisect on a small sample of tests if you feel like it. That'll help you know where the regression happened instead of fixing and running the whole thing. But ofc only do that if it's useful!
You're a rock star! Just to understand, are there 0 regressions?
You're a rock star! Just to understand, are there 0 regressions?
You're a rock star! Just to understand, are there 0 regressions?
You're a rock star! Just to understand, are there 0 regressions?
Great! Is everything pushed to GitHub?
Please commit and plush those, and if any issues are not closed, please close them.
